current.title: The Happy Prince
current.author: Oscar Wilde
outline: High above the city stands the gilded statue of the Happy Prince, admired by everyone. A little swallow, weeks behind his friends who have already flown to Egypt, stops to sleep between the statue's feet — and drops of water fall on him from a cloudless sky. He looks up: the Happy Prince is weeping.

The Prince explains that in life he lived in a palace where sorrow could not enter, and only now, raised above the city, can he see all its misery. He begs the swallow to carry the ruby from his sword-hilt to a poor seamstress whose little son lies ill with fever. The swallow, though longing for Egypt, agrees to stay one night; after the errand he feels strangely warm despite the cold.

Night after night the Prince finds new suffering. He makes the swallow pluck out one sapphire eye for a young playwright too cold and hungry to finish his play, and then — over the bird's protests — the other eye for a little match-girl who has lost her matches and dares not go home. The Prince is now blind, and the swallow resolves to stay with him always.

The swallow flies over the city and tells the blind Prince what he sees: the rich making merry while beggars sit at the gates, children starving in dark lanes. Leaf by leaf, the swallow strips the Prince's fine gold and carries it to the poor. The statue grows grey and shabby; winter arrives, and the little bird grows colder and weaker.

Knowing he is dying, the swallow kisses the Happy Prince and falls dead at his feet — and inside the statue, the leaden heart cracks in two. The shabby statue is pulled down and melted, but the broken heart will not melt and is thrown on the dust-heap beside the dead bird. When God asks for the two most precious things in the city, an angel brings Him the leaden heart and the dead swallow.
